speaker: interviewee
text: And so we started our life in the little ghetto, that was to be our home for another couple of years. This was the summer of 1942. My dad and my brother Joe were the first ones to venture out into the countryside, and they were lucky enough that they found a Ukrainian lady who was a doctor veterinarian. And she started giving my dad and my brother odd jobs around her place-- chopping wood and working on the-- on the potato patch and--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know that you're not allowed to be there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it was, of course, very dangerous to do that. But like I said we were a little-- or my dad was and I was-- we were little more comfortable because we could speak the language and we weren't afraid to be out in the countryside and to do work on, on gardening, and all that kind of stuff.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it dangerous for her to let you work for her?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I, I don't think so, because it went on for some time. And she was very glad that my dad and my brother Joe were doing the work for her. And maybe she was the kind of person that tried to help us out as well. She might have been from those Ukrainians that were feeling sorry for us and tried to help us.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you get in return for the work?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were given food while they were working and maybe something to take home, a half a loaf of bread, a few potatoes, anything. Food was starting to get a little scarce because as time went on the Ukrainian population wasn't as willing to come into town because word spread that there was disease spreading in the ghetto. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me just stop you for a second. What made that a ghetto? Why do you call it a ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: I call it a ghetto because it was a area that was surrounded, populated, overpopulated with Jewish people. And the Jewish people weren't allowed to go anywhere except to stay within that border.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the purpose of them staying in that border?

speaker: interviewee
text: The purpose was that the Romanians wanted to see those people starve or die of disease.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you still call that a ghetto?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I call it a ghetto. I don't know why I'm connecting the word ghetto to me a place that's confined and deprived of the freedom of moving around outside of it. It's like a concentration camp. But in that respect it was more like a ghetto because we didn't have no barbed wires surrounding us. So in a way it was a cross between a ghetto and a concentration camp. I don't know, maybe ghetto isn't the right word. But I'm referring to it as a ghetto. Anyway, that's how he had lived. And life started to settle in, and people started to get used to their life, as bad as it was and as dangerous as it was. But people had to go on.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to the other families in the hut where you lived?

speaker: interviewee
text: They--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they work, too, for food?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, my uncle-- my uncle you talking about?

speaker: interviewer
text: The other people who lived in that hut with you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, well, of course, they were local people, and they may have had some provisions. But they, too, had to barter for food. And they may have had some connections with other Ukrainian people that were trying to help them out. Everybody struggled in his own way as best as he could to survive. A lot of people, Jewish people, weren't prepared for a struggle. And I refer mostly to the intellectuals, to the professionals that had had a very easy life before the war started. And those people weren't equipped to cope, and so a lot of them gave up and died. Also there were a lot of older people and weak people that, again, weren't physically strong enough or mentally prepared to go out and, and do what they could in order to survive. A lot of them turned to beggars.

speaker: interviewer
text: From whom did they beg?

speaker: interviewee
text: They begged. They begged. They walked the streets and begged. And sometimes somebody would find it in their heart to share with them whatever they had. Life started to be very difficult and cruel.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year are we in now, Leizer?

speaker: interviewee
text: We are at the beginning of the winter of '43-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] -- '42. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] Let me just think-- let me just think exactly. We were summer of '42, winter of '42, '43. That was the worst winter of the 3 and 1/2 years that we spent in that concentration camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: What made it the worst?

speaker: interviewee
text: Two things-- people started running out of food or things to barter for food and disease started to be very rampant-- dysentery, typhus, malnutrition, terrible lice and the sight of the wagons constantly hauling away corpses. All a sudden your neighbor was gone.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were the people who were hauling away the corpses?

speaker: interviewee
text: Must have been local Ukrainian people that were in charge or were paid to do this.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who paid them?

speaker: interviewee
text: It must have been the-- either the Jewish community or, or-- it must have been the Jewish community. I wasn't aware of all these things, as to who was in charge or who was doing  the pay. We did have-- and we knew about a centralized organization that Jewish people that were in charge of the community. And they supervised or oversaw the police work outside of--

speaker: interviewer
text: Whose police work?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were Jewish police. They were Jewish police. Or I remember a name by the name of Zant, Bruno Zant, I think it was. He was in charge of police. Now, I, I don't know exactly whether they were Jewish police, but all the authority they had was to round up Jews to serve in the labor gangs for the Romanians or for the whatever groups organized labor, forced labor.

speaker: interviewer
text: So what they did, the Romanians, they went to the Jewish local authorities and told them, we need so many Jewish men for work. And they went out to secure those men for the Romanians. Do you remember any names from the administration of that Jewish committee?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember there was a Dr. Tich, and, and that Zant comes to mind. And I don't remember of any other names. Anyway, that winter our family all took sick of typhus, and it was a dreadful disease. The hospitals, whatever they were-- one or two hospitals-- they were all filled up. And there was hardly any medication. There was no doctors to care for anybody. There was some people that had a little bit of knowledge or experience that were in charge of these things, Jewish people. They weren't given much to do, but they did what they could. Then, of course, people were dying. We were fortunate enough, all four of us, to survive the typhus. In the house we lived, Simon died of typhus. And the oldest man in the family, of the local-- of the Ukrainian family or Jewish family that took us in, he died as well. But everybody in the house had typhus.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you eat then, you couldn't go out to work?

speaker: interviewee
text: During the time that we were sick, I remember only having some hot water, or hot tea, what you may call. And that's all. We had no food. During the time that we were sick, the fever was very high-- 42, or 43, or even 44. And you go on like that for a week or several days until the fever breaks. And at the point when it breaks, most of the people can't, can't survive the shock, so they die. We survived. My uncle Simcha, my dad's brother, took sick. And he died, of course. When I came out of my typhus, I heard that he died. And he was the first one from our family and my favorite uncle, and I could not accept the fact that he died. I couldn't deal with it. But no sooner I could crawl or walk, I went over to where I knew he lived. And I found my aunt, and my cousins, and some other distant relatives in that little room. And they were all sick. And they were all dying. My uncle died. They took his corpse away. But all the other corpses-- it was in the middle of winter-- they piled all those people in a large pile by another old synagogue that was there for hundreds of years. They said from the time of the Turks.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he was gone. And when I walked into their house I asked my aunt what I could do, what they would like, and what can I do to help them. So I found an old pair of my uncle shoes, and I went to the market and bartered it for a little bit of milk and whatever I could. And I brought that to them.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I left it with them and I crawled back. I say crawled because I could hardly walk. I could make three, four steps, and then I would have to sit down and crawl. And that's how I got back to our home or our place where we lived. The winter was very, very cold. A lot of people were naked, freezing to death.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they get naked?

speaker: interviewee
text: They simply bartered away everything they had, and they had nothing left for themselves. Most of the people were, including myself and our family, make clothes out of old sacks, potato sacks. And that's what we wore. Were covered with sacks. We were emaciated. My younger brother, Joe, took very sick, and they took him to a hospital, which was not far from where we lived. And I remember I had starting to get a little better, and I used to go to the hospital. And I wasn't allowed to do that, but I used to go every day and look through the window. There was a window. I used to go to the window, crawl over a wall, go to the window to just see him, and, and to make sure that he saw me. And when he came out of his disease, he survived in that hospital. He wasn't given much, but he survived it. My dad went to get him, and he carried him home on his back because he couldn't walk.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why was he taken to the hospital and the rest of you were not?

speaker: interviewee
text: They allowed some people to go to the hospital, and they made provision for that one, for him, to go there. I really don't know, but I-- that's what I remember. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] After we survived the typhus in that terrible winter, I remember in the spring of that year, everything was gone. We had nothing left. We had nothing left to barter. There was no food. I remember my dad one day [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] telling us, kinderlekh, [SOBBING], I can no longer do anything for you. We have no food. We got nothing left. I don't know. He said, I don't know whether I just got out of typhus. He was deaf. He couldn't hear. He was a broken man. He was a broken man. He said, you kids to us do whatever you can do if you want to survive. If you have to be beggars, be beggars. But nope. My younger brother and I, [SOBBING], we said, no, we are not going to be beggars. And we started venturing out into the country again. And little by little we were strong enough to start doing some work, and we were given some food.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go back to the same lady?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went to the same countryside and to some of the same people, and they were kind to us. We, of course, we weren't scared to work. And we were good workers, and they knew us. And, like I said before, I have to say that a lot of the Ukrainian local people were feeling sorry for us. Just as there were many that were out and out killers, there were some that were honest and good human beings. Of course, they, too, started to feel the effects of the Romanian and the German occupation. However, we started to struggle, and we didn't want to give up. And then my dad got a little stronger, and he, too, started going out again. And so [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] we started hearing of a reversal of maybe the war starting to change in favor of the Russians.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you hear that?

speaker: interviewee
text: We heard that because the Romanians had newspapers. And they allowed, once in a while, newspapers to come into to Shargorod and to the other areas and word spread. It's amazing how sometimes these things spread without anybody knowing how they spread. But it's like a wind that brings news, brings music from a distance. And we heard that the Russians were getting close to Warsaw. They were getting close to Kiev. And Germans were facing defeats in, in many areas. And we were starting to hope that maybe someday we might be a liberated again, and we might survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Transnistria or in Shargorod, did you see Romanian soldiers, or German soldiers, Ukrainian soldiers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mostly Romanian soldiers. When they started retreat, then we started to see all three of them-- Romanians, Germans, and Ukrainians.

speaker: interviewer
text: Soldiers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Soldiers. And it, it-- towards the winter of '44, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] news really started coming more and more to the fact that the Russians were advancing. At the same time, we were given to understand that when the Russians-- when the Germans or the Romanians retreat, they massacre the local Jewish population. So on the one hand, we were--

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean just the Ukrainian?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Romanians and the Ukrainians.

speaker: interviewer
text: No, who would they massacre?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jewish population. The Jewish population--

speaker: interviewer
text: Everybody?

speaker: interviewee
text: Whatever Jews were alive were in danger of being massacred. They didn't want to leave anybody alive. And, indeed, on the other side of the Bug, of course, that's what happened. They massacred all the Jewish people on the other side of the Bug.

speaker: interviewee
text: On the Romanian side, somehow the Romanians, as bad as they were, they were smart enough to realize that if the Germans lose they'll be faced with having to account for what they did. And the queen, the Romanian queen, and the influence she had from the-- from the local Jewish people that were in charge of the Jewish community-- a Dr. Federer? Filderman.

speaker: interviewee
text: Filderman-- they impressed on the queen to somehow try and see that the Romanians don't commit the same atrocities as the Germans. And the Romanians realized, too, that maybe the Germans were going to lose the war. So things started to change.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you get any help from the Jewish communities left in Romania?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I remember that salt was an item which wasn't available. And in Romania there were a lot of salt. And for a wagon of salt you could barter for food and from a lot of stuff. So they sent salt. They sent money. They did what they could to help. And it saved a lot of people.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did that get to the people? Through the Jewish committee?

speaker: interviewee
text: Through some of the Jewish organized communities, such as it was. Jewish people in all the circumstances that they were, whichever extermination camps they were in, under the worst circumstances, they maintained a level of civility, a level of trying to be normal in a very abnormal surroundings and circumstances. People davened. People fasted--

speaker: interviewer
text: For holidays, you mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: --on Yom Kippur. On Yom Kippur. How do you fast when you have nothing to eat anyway? But you can do it. You can pretend that you have a little bite just before evening sets in, and that's the signal for fasting. In the ghetto of Shargorod, I understand there was an orphanage. And I remember one of my other uncles that was also in Shargorod. I forgot to mention his name. His name was Ushe, Ushe Zailich. He was from Czernowitz. He organized the orphanage to perform and sing Jewish songs. So here we are in an extermination camp. We are dying. We see corpses. We see starvation and disease. And we also hear a Jewish song, a Jewish love song.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did they perform, the kids?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember there was a building where there used to be a soup kitchen as well. And it was in that area that this was happening. Because I once went and heard the songs. I didn't go to the soup kitchen. I don't know somehow we never went to the-- I don't remember going to the soup kitchen. Maybe I didn't even know there was a soup kitchen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me-- I meant to ask you then and I forgot. What happens to these hundreds of thousands of corpses that were gathered every day?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's a good question. When they piled those corpses in the winter time and then they tried to dislodge them in the spring before it melted, all those corpses froze together. And they became one huge, big lump. And I was told, and we all knew, that they made people chop them apart with axes and with whatever tools they gave them so that they could bury them and bury them before it got warm. And they were scared for an epidemic.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were they buried?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a cemetery outside of Shargorod, several kilometers. And I think they took all these remains and buried them out there. And that's where--

speaker: interviewer
text: In a grave?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, there were graves. There were graves. There was-- it was a cemetery. Well, I don't know. They must have put them all into a mass grave. But there was a cemetery out there. And I think all the people that died were taken out to that cemetery. And so the, the winter of '44-- because we heard that maybe the Russians can't be far away and the Germans might retreating to the Romanians-- that maybe we were faced with extermination by the Romanians massacring us before they leave. So everybody started to think of maybe building some hiding places. And I remember we had built a hiding place with some other people. And we used to-- we were very worried, and I remember, many nights watching. Everybody slept and one would watch. I remember watching. I remember the moon shining, and the stars, and the snow, and the ice, and the dark. And you watch. You watch.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you hide?

speaker: interviewee
text: The hiding place-- we didn't even go. We made a hiding place. We practiced a couple of times. It was a false place. Any kind of dog or anybody could have found us easy, but we thought maybe it was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, where was it?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was underneath, underneath in a cellar. We dug another hole in there underneath with our false wall. So it was done as best as we could. It would house maybe-- it would hold maybe 10, 15 people.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you-- would you get air in there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't even remember, to be honest with you.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you actually never had to use them?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we never had to use it. We never had to use it. The, the-- we suddenly heard, towards the end of the winter of '44, that the Russians were really advancing. And we started to see the first signs of the Germans retreating.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were they?

speaker: interviewee
text: We'd see the odd group of German soldiers simply going back, going from the front rather than to the front. A lot of Russian prisoners of war, which were being evacuated, also back. The feeling was we kind of sensed that the end of the occupation was near. Partisans, partisans started being very active in the surrounding areas. Once in awhile, they'd venture into town and kill a Ukrainian authority or a Romanian authority.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: They killed one of the local Ukrainian officials. It wasn't very far from where we lived, and we heard about it. The whole time-- the whole time we would see a partisan in chains being led by the Romanian soldiers, and we only knew what his fate was going to be. We also heard that the local Jewish people were making contact with some of the Partisans and making arrangements that when the evacuation would happen, that perhaps they could forestall a massacre.

speaker: interviewer
text: What evacuation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like when the Romanians evacuated the last authorities that the partisans would forestall or see that a massacre doesn't take place.

speaker: interviewer
text: How would they have done that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I don't know what kind of arrangements were, but we heard about it. And, indeed, after the war we found out that there had been such arrangements made, whether it would have helped or not, I don't know. But it was nice to know that there was somebody trying to do something to save us from what it looked like, a definite massacre. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] And we started hoping that maybe our liberation was near.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, this is a good place to start. Thank you. To stop, I mean.
